intro.kicker: Topic 10 of 11 • Chapter: Motion In One Dimension • Unit: Kinematics • Physics
intro.topicName: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall)
intro.description: This topic covers Free Fall - Body Dropped from Height, Body Projected Vertically Downward, Body Projected Vertically Upward and Motion with Air Resistance. Key principles include: Speed of return equals speed of projection; Magnitude of velocity at any height is same whether moving up or down. In NEET Physics, Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) is tested directly and as part of multi-concept problems. Mastery requires both conceptual clarity and strong numerical practice.

expectedQuestions.description: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) features in NEET Physics as a direct question or embedded in multi-step problems. Detailed study of Motion In One Dimension is recommended.

timeRequired.description: Theory 10–15 min, plus solving 30 practice problems. Efficient if you know the formula derivation.

difficulty.description: Motion In One Dimension difficulty level. Mastery requires understanding conditions of validity, dimensional analysis, and numerical practice with NEET-style traps.

nriUsaGap.description: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) has overlap with AP Physics curriculum, though NEET requires deeper formula derivation and multi-step numerical mastery beyond standard AP Physics expectations.

cards.title: NEET Traps to Avoid
cards.coreRevision: NEET setters target conceptual gaps in Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall). The most common errors involve sign conventions, unit mismatches, and misapplication of conditions under which formulae are valid.

gaps.title: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) in US vs Indian Curriculum
gaps.description: AP Physics 1 covers kinematics but with less emphasis on graph interpretation depth and relative velocity problems found in Indian physics syllabi.
bullets: NEET expects derivation-level mastery; AP Physics often treats Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) as applied only.
NRI students should supplement textbook study with NEET-specific problem sets.
Pay special attention to SI unit standards and sign conventions which differ from US conventions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) in the context of NEET Physics?

options.text: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) applies only under idealized conditions with no friction or air resistance
Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) is a fundamental result derivable from first principles and applies within well-defined physical conditions
Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) has no mathematical formulation and is purely qualitative
Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) contradicts Newton's laws in non-inertial frames

questions.explanation: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) is a rigorously derived physical principle within Motion In One Dimension. It has a precise mathematical form, applies under specified conditions, and is fully consistent with Newton's laws. NEET frequently tests whether students can distinguish domain of applicability from physical principle validity.

questions.question: In the study of Motion In One Dimension, which statement about Body Projected Vertically Downward is CORRECT for a NEET Physics student?

options.text: It is only tested as an isolated topic and never combined with energy or momentum problems
The dimensional formula does not follow from SI base units
It is integral to Motion In One Dimension and appears in direct and multi-concept NEET questions
It is outside the NEET Physics syllabus since 2020

questions.explanation: Body Projected Vertically Downward is a core component of Motion In One Dimension and regularly appears in NEET. Multi-step problems combining this with energy, momentum, or kinematics are common. Dimensional analysis confirms SI unit consistency. This topic has remained in the NEET syllabus continuously.

questions.answer: Whenever the stated conditions for Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems

items.question: How many questions from Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) appear in NEET each year?
items.answer: Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) typically contributes 0–2 questions per NEET paper depending on the year. While it may not appear every year, it often features in multi-concept problems linked to adjacent topics in Motion In One Dimension. Skipping it is risky.

items.question: Is Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) in the NEET 2025 syllabus?
items.answer: Yes, Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) remains part of the NEET Physics syllabus under Motion In One Dimension. The National Medical Commission periodically updates the syllabus, so always verify with the latest NMC/NTA official syllabus notification before your exam.

items.question: What is the best way to revise Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) before NEET?
items.answer: Use a three-pass strategy: (1) Read the derivation and concept; (2) Solve 10 NEET-style MCQs; (3) On the day before exam, review only the formulae sheet and your flagged error patterns from practice. Focus on conditions of validity and sign conventions.

items.question: What is the most common mistake in Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) problems?
items.answer: The most frequent errors in Motion of Body Under Gravity (Free Fall) are: applying the formula outside its domain (e.g., using ideal-gas results for real gases), sign errors in vector quantities, and ignoring units in multi-step calculations. Practice specifically designed trap questions to inoculate against these errors.
